data.title: SOXX's AI Premium Must Be Paid - Near Term Overbought Risks
data.description:
I previously covered VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH) in January 2026, discussing its diversified exposure to the multi-year AI spending trends.

SOXX Proves Its AI Beneficiary Status

Similar to SMH, iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX) is another semiconductor-focused ETF that has been a beneficiary during the ongoing data center boom

While past performance does not guarantee future results, SOXX has delivered an excellent price return of +210.2% since the launch of ChatGPT by OpenAI (OPENAI) on November 30, 2022.

This is compared to the wider market at +70.3% over the same time period.

1. Robust Exposure To Cloud Super Cycle

Much of SOXX's outperformance is attributed to their somewhat balanced weightage for their top few holdings, such as the top holding at an 8.4% weightage compared to the top 5 averages at ~7.1%.

This strategy allows the ETF to enjoy a diversified exposure into the peaks and troughs of the ongoing AI boom, as observed in NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) in H1'24/Q2'25, Broadcom, Inc. (AVGO) in December 2024/Q2'25, and Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) in H2'25.

Most importantly, SOXX's top holdings are focused on the winners of the AI race, namely:

Nvidia

As the general AI accelerator market leader, NVDA has delivered excellent data center revenue growth, from $14.99 billion in FY2023 to $193.72 billion in FY2026, expanding at a 3Y CAGR of +134.6%.

This is while hinting at "$1 trillion plus of Blackwell plus Rubin" revenues at the end of 2027, up from the prior guidance of $500 billion, with it underscoring the durable data center spending trends entering 2026/2027.

Broadcom

Broadcom has emerged as the custom AI accelerator market leader, with the robust hyperscaler demand already observed in their AI semiconductor revenue growth from $4.2 billion in FY2023 (15% of FY2023 semiconductor revenues) to an annualized sum of $33.6 billion in FQ1'26, expanding at a 2Y CAGR of +182.8%.

The management has also guided an excellent "AI revenue from chips, just chips, in excess of $100 billion in 2027," with it implying an excellent growth profile at a 2Y CAGR of +123.6% from FY2025 levels of $20 billion.

This development is unsurprising indeed, given Broadcom's growing hyperscaler base through Alphabet Inc.'s (GOOG) TPU, ByteDance (BDNCE), Meta Platforms, Inc.'s (META) MTIA, an unnamed customer who previously ordered "$10 billion of orders of AI racks," OpenAI, and two more hyperscalers in development stages.

This is on top of Anthropic (ANTHRO) also signing multiple gigawatts of GOOG's next-generation TPU capacity, as similarly observed with META, with these developments likely allowing Broadcom to raise their FY2027 guidance beyond the prior number of $100 billion.

Micron

With "AI workloads requiring large amounts of memory... AI servers and enterprise environments require far more memory per system than consumer devices," MU has been able to successfully monetize their DRAM and NAND market leadership while delivering a much-needed reversal from the prior memory destruction in 2023.

The same has been embedded in its excellent revenue growth from $15.54 billion in FY2023 to the estimated FY2026 at $104.5 billion (H1'25 at $37.5 billion, the FQ3'26 guidance at $33.5 billion, and the FQ4'26 assumption of $33.5 billion), expanding at an estimated 3Y CAGR of +88.7%.

Combined with their ongoing capacity expansions in numerous locations, the hyperscalers' demand gated by supply, and the ongoing price hikes, it is unsurprising that the consensus already expects MU to deliver an outsized top-line growth in a CAGR of +61.1% through FY2028.

Advanced Micro Devices

This is not forgetting the two other stocks in the top 5 holdings, including Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD), which already reported robust data center revenue growth from $6.04 billion in FY2022 to $16.6 billion in FY2025, expanding at a 3Y CAGR of +40%.

Thanks to its diversified growth prospects across the embedded semiconductor (Microsoft's Xbox) and x86 CPU end market, it is unsurprising that the management has offered an ambitious Analyst Day 2025 revenue growth target at a CAGR of +35% and adj EPS growth at +40% over the next few years.

Marvell (MRVL)

As the designer for Amazon's and Microsoft's custom AI chips, Marvell Technology, Inc. (MRVL) has also experienced robust data center revenue growth, from $2.4 billion in FY2023 to $6.09 billion in FY2026, expanding at a 3Y CAGR of +36.4%.

With AMZN already guiding outsized CapEx of $200 billion in FY2026 (+55.8% YoY) and Microsoft announcing numerous data center-related projects over the past few months, I am of the opinion that MRVL is well positioned to deliver their prior FY2027 data center revenue growth guidance by +40% YoY and FY2028 by +50% YoY.

2. Growth Premium Already Embedded

As a result of their holdings' robust, multi-year growth prospects, I can understand why SOXX has been able to enjoy an outsized price rally since the March 2026 bottom, as similarly observed in the wider market and its semiconductor-focused ETFs in varying degrees.

Beyond the top five holdings, readers may also want to note the ETF's strategic focus on US-based stocks, allowing them to mitigate the risks from future geopolitical and/or tariff issues.

This is on top of the benefit of SOXX's elevated exposure to the leading fabless names at 76.1%, with it mitigating the free cash flow headwinds from the foundries' likely to be elevated CapEx trends.

The +29.8% rally over the past two weeks is also why SOXX appears to be somewhat inflated at a P/E of 43.73x, compared to the wider market at 39.08x.

Even then, I am of the opinion that the ETF remains a Great Buy, since its top five holdings continue to trade near to their 5Y P/E means, including:

  • Advanced Micro Devices at a FWD P/E of 38.13x against the 5Y mean of 37.48x and
  • Marvell at 35.01x/36.45x,

aside from:

  • Nvidia at the currently discounted P/E of 23.66x against the 5Y mean of 44.63x and
  • Micron at 8.06x/74.46x,

along with Broadcom's current premium at 33.41x/25.74x, respectively.

Combined with the holdings' high double/triple-digit top/bottom-line growth prospects over the next few years, I believe that SOXX may deliver robust capital appreciation beyond the ongoing rally since the April 2025 bottom.

This is especially since the prior AI pessimism has already been exhausted during the worst of the March 2026 selloff, as the market enters the Q1'26 earnings season afresh in April 2026.

SOXX ETF Is A Buy After A Dip

For now, SOXX is retesting a new high at the $401s after the recent breakout, with it remaining to be seen if the upward momentum may be sustained after the nearly vertical rally.

This is especially since SOXX has been overbought, as observed in the elevated RSIs, with the uncertain macroeconomic and geopolitical environment potentially triggering a profit-taking cadence at current inflated levels.

This is especially since the Iran conflict is still developing. Pending an actual resolution and the consistent flow in the global oil/gas supply, I believe that the inflationary pressure may worsen from current levels, with March 2026's somewhat muted numbers likely to give way to worse numbers in April 2026.

This is especially since the broader chip supply chain constraint may negate the promising global chip sales cadence observed in February 2026, pending the foundries' further capacity expansions.

With market analysts already expecting the Fed to hold interest rates steady in the upcoming FOMC meeting, my initial Buy rating comes with the caveat that interested investors wait for a moderate correction before adding, preferably between the January/February 2026 tops of $360 and the 50-day moving averages of $250s.

Those entry points may offer an improved margin of safety while allowing investors to partake in the structurally robust, multi-year AI supercycle.

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway: The Fortress Has Become A Waiting Room
data.description:
Introduction

I last covered Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.A) (BRK.B) back in August with a dose of skepticism on the back of the potential Warren Buffett premium eroding. Since the company’s stock has only appreciated 1.94% since Buffett officially retired, I believe that my rating was on point. Now under new CEO Greg Abel, I want to dive into the company’s path forward and whether it has an undervaluation thesis.

Current Dynamics

The biggest dynamic has been Greg Abel assuming the CEO position while Warren's son Howard Buffett assumed the position of Non-Executive Chairman, a role that will be tailored to keep the same corporate culture and prevent short-term institutional decay. The transition has introduced a more institutionalized and team-oriented management style, unlike the historical style of Buffett, which Abel has diverted from while signing a collaborative approach and delegating significant oversight.

In his inaugural letter, Abel also diverted from the folksy and aphoristic wit of his predecessor with a more direct and conservative narrative with a focus on operation. Abel has emphasized three core pillars: stewardship, capital discipline, and the maintenance of the fortress balance sheet. Abel has already demonstrated a more active, albeit still decentralized, management style, asking tougher questions regarding operational efficiency and capital returns. This has been evident in his public critique of BNSF Railway and the utilities under Berkshire Hathaway Energy.

Looking at the broad numbers for 2025, operating earnings have declined by 6.2% to $44.5B, which was largely due to the normalization of the insurance cycle and specific headwinds in the energy and railroad segments. The decline in insurance underwriting earnings was majorly pronounced in Q4, where results fell by nearly 30%, with the weakness driven by a combination of higher catastrophe losses and a deceleration in the pricing power that had boosted margins in 2023 and 2024. Though the manufacturing service and retailing segment showed resilience, thanks to brands like See’s Candies and Dairy Queen.

On the cash front, it remains one of the biggest sources of contemplation, as it now stands at $373.3B at year-end. The level of liquidity is unprecedented, which represents nearly 40% of the firm’s total market capitalization. The bulk of this position is deployed in short-term US treasury bills totaling $321.4B. This reserve is based on two major dynamics that have been noticed by Buffett since the COVID pandemic: the lack of attractive large-scale acquisition opportunities and a cautious view on the broader market valuations. By hoarding such a high level of cash, Berkshire is positioned to act as a lender of last resort or the potential buyer of first choice should a major correction occur.

On the portfolio side, the cash that was diverted away from the Apple (AAPL) stake has been partially redeployed into Alphabet (GOOG) as Berkshire revealed a massive new entry into the firm in Q3 of 2025 as it purchased 17.8MM shares valued at around $4.3B. The investment team viewed Alphabet as a premier play on the integration of GenAI into existing platform ecosystems. I believe that it is the right move, especially as it trimmed the position of Apple and Amazon to hedge an all-around bet on the new tech wave. Meaning that the firm Berkshire is recalibrating its tech exposure toward platform-level AI dominance rather than consumer hardware.

On the industrial front, there are massive structural challenges facing Greg Abel. BNSF has underperformed its primary rival, Union Pacific (UNP), in terms of operating ratio and profitability. The internal struggle has now been compounded by a massive threat that is the proposed mega-merger between UNP and Norfolk Southern (NSC). This would create the first truly transcontinental railroad in U.S. history, with a network spanning over 52,000 miles across 43 states. This would essentially put BNSF at a structural disadvantage.

For Berkshire Hathaway Energy, which was once considered the crown jewel of the non-insurance businesses, and is currently navigating a period of heightened risk. Operating earnings stood at $3.98B, which is a modest increase but still overshadowed by massive contingent liabilities. The main one being the large liability exposure related to the wildfires in the western part of the U.S., mainly affecting the PacificCorp subsidiary.

Though despite the challenges, BHE continues to expand its industrial footprint with the completion of the deal for OxyChem, the chemical arm of Occidental Petroleum. This acquisition fits the Berkshire model for acquiring businesses with regulated or contracted cash flows and significant asset bases. The move will further integrate it with Berkshire’s energy portfolio, which includes the 27% stake in Oxy (OXY) as well as the 6.5% stake in Chevron (CVX).

Valuation

Valuing Berkshire on financial metrics or compared to peers is not really possible given its one-of-a-kind status in terms of companies. That’s why I did an SOTP, DCF, and scenario-based analysis. On an SOTP view, the total equity value, taking into account the private businesses, the cash position, and the equity investment portfolio, would amount to $1.15T, or $797,450 per Class A share and $531.63 per Class B share, or an 11.3% upside. Though I’ll say that in a scenario-based analysis, even the bear case only amounts to a slim downside of -0.5%, giving a certain floor to the stock price. On a probability-weighted Class B price, this would imply a stock price of $537.27, still a fair upside from here in twelve months' time.

On a DCF front, though, my total per Class B share would come to $428.87, which would be a 10.2% downside, but Berkshire is a staple of protection in case of a downturn in the economy. Though many could argue that Berkshire, due to its size, is now a proxy for the overall economy, so it might be hard to grow at my assumed CAGR.

There are also certain developments I'm closely looking at, as there is a growing speculation that Berkshire may eventually use its cash hoard to repurchase the massive block of shares held by Buffett’s estate after they donated to his foundation. This would prevent a massive overhang of shares on the market and serve as the largest repurchase in corporate history. I’ll also look forward to the Japanese expansion as BRK continues to deepen its ties to the Japanese economy, as the five major trading houses are now seen as the fourth-pillar holdings comparable in importance to Apple and American Express (AXP).

Risks

The Buffett premium has now largely evaporated, as quantified by the compression in the price-to-book ratio from 1.75x to 1.5x, representing roughly $150B in lost market value attributable to the leadership transition. That was the main point in my past analysis, and now we still don’t know how the new stewardship will be able to regain such a premium and if it can replicate the market-moving influence the firm once had.

There are also the key challenges that subsidiaries are facing with BNSF Railway, as well as the Insurance Underwriting, in which Greg Abel has warned will continue to see headwinds during the year.

Conclusion

I’ll say that the core dynamic of the firm remains intact, with the insurance float providing an unparalleled source of low-cost capital, while the industrial side provides a resilient cash flow base. But the challenge arises from the leadership transition needing to prove to the market that it can push Berkshire into a new successful era without the Oracle of Omaha. I still want to wait and see if it is worth buying shares at this point. I keep my Hold rating on the firm.

data.title: This Rally Won't End Well
data.description:
Thesis Summary

The S&P 500 is pushing back towards 7,000, and a lot of investors are finding this very surprising.

Oil is up 60% YTD, the Iran conflict is ongoing, inflation is heating up, and there’s little help expected to come from the Fed.

So what’s going on exactly?

In this article, I’ll discuss why I believe the market has been so resilient over the last few weeks.

Following this, we’ll dive into exactly why this might be the time you want to take some chips off the table and specifically how you could implement this through options.

Why Are Markets Not Down?

This is an intriguing question, and one we can’t really answer with 100% certainty. We can, however, make some educated guesses.

Positioning And Flows

The thing about markets is that they don’t always move in the direction you’d expect based on headlines, and that’s often due to positioning.

In this particular scenario, the Iran war headlines created a situation in which market participants were very well hedged.

This is reflected in the increase in Short ETF Volume.

When there is so much shorting, market makers have to hedge these short positions, which actually makes them go long.

And of course, if the price does eventually move up, this can then be accelerated even further by a short squeeze.

Positioning also ties into market flows, which have also created a situation where CTAs are underweight the market, and due to specific price signals, they have to buy into the market.

On the flow side, Goldman models Commodity Trading Advisors as buyers of roughly $45B in U.S. equities over the next week, with approximately $34B of that concentrated in the S&P.

Source: SA Press Release

These CTAs also add fuel to the fire because they have to chase rallies once momentum shifts.

Market Liquidity

Liquidity is another factor to take into account. Tax refunds from the BBB are going into consumer accounts, and that’s creating a tailwind for the economy.

Since the Iran conflict began, there’s an argument to be made that $700 billion of stimulus-like liquidity has supported the system.

Once we add tax refunds, government spending, and also the slight expansion of the Fed balance sheet.

AI Acceleration

Lastly, the war has done little to stop the popularity of the AI trade, which we can’t deny is being supported by the data.

Demand seems to be increasing across the board. GPU prices are up, there’s not enough memory, and the hyperscalers continue to roll out more investment.

Pair this with the fact that valuations for a lot of the Mag 7 were becoming quite attractive, and you have a perfect recipe for buyers to step in.

Why I Expect A Further Pull-Back

But all of this doesn’t mean the market isn’t going to roll over further. There’s a lot of good reasons to expect weakness.

War Escalation

While peace talks are ongoing, the geopolitical risk remains, and we continue to see escalation on the ground and sea.

The US has moved to blockade Iranian ports, and the latter has now threatened to retaliate if this continues.

And while oil prices have come off their highs, it does seem like they are stabilizing at near $100, which is bad news if they stay at these levels.

Inflation

Inflation is now the highest it has been in two years, and this is going to be challenging for stocks.

If we get a couple more hot prints, then tech stocks, especially, are going to get hit hard.

Seasonality

Let’s also not forget that this is an election year. Midterms come with added volatility, and seasonality data also shows that historically, this is the worst time in terms of market performance.

Earnings Volatility

We’ve also seen this play out before during previous earnings cycles.

We rally into earnings, and even though most companies beat, we see big sell-offs across the board and increased volatility.

I think a lot of companies are going to get hit hard this earnings season, too, and give back a lot of their recent gains.

How To Hedge With A Put Spread

So, how do we play this?

Personally, I still want to stay invested in a lot of the AI names, and I also think accumulating Mag 7 at these lower valuations is a good move.

However, I think at the current level in the VIX, a put spread could be used to hedge the downside risk.

For example, you could buy a slightly out-of-the-money put, around 5% below today's levels, and then sell a deeper out-of-the-money put, perhaps somewhere in the 10% correction level.

This structure keeps your cost around ~1% of notional value and will act as insurance.

The key is that you’re not just betting on price falling; you’re also positioned to benefit if volatility spikes, which seems like a likely scenario.

If we do indeed see a mild correction of 5%, you could realistically see a 2–3x return on that premium, while a sharper 10% drawdown and a VIX spike into the 25–35 range could push returns closer to 5–6x.

A 5% sell-off would put us back at the 200-day EMA, while a 10% sell-off would essentially be a revisit of the previous lows.

Both are key areas you want to watch.

Of course, if nothing happens and the market grinds higher, you simply lose the premium, but that’s the cost of hedging.

Final Thoughts

Ultimately, I think the next two to three months could hold significant downside, and today’s price and VIX give us a good entry to put some shorts on and hedge our portfolio.

data.title: H&R Block Q3 Preview: Price Correction Is A Blessing For The Long-Term Investor
data.description:
H&R Block (HRB) will be reporting one of the most crucial earnings quarters on May 6th (estimated). That is where most of the money will be made, as it will cover most of the tax season. I wanted to take a look at the expectations in more detail, and seeing that the company’s share price is significantly down since my last article over a year ago, I thought it would be a good time to revisit to see if the company is a buy at these levels now.

What to Expect

So, for earnings, analysts are expecting the company to make around $5.63 and $5.77 on a GAAP and adjusted basis, respectively, on around $2.34B in revenues. This translates to around 6% growth in GAAP earnings and around 3% growth in sales. Nothing spectacular, but I am not expecting anything big from a tax enterprise.

Unfortunately, the company didn’t provide any concrete guidance for the upcoming quarter, but they did reaffirm fiscal 2026 guidance after the last report. The company kept its full-year outlook at $3.875-$3.895B in revenue and $4.85-$5.00 in adjusted EPS. The best way to get a comparison is to see what the analysts are expecting the company’s full-year performance to be. As we can see below, estimates are largely in line with the management.

And what about the upcoming quarter and whether the company will beat or miss estimates on this crucial tax season quarter? We can see that, over the last ten quarters, the company beat revenues 90% of the time and beat EPS 80% of the time. Regarding Q3s, the company hasn’t missed once.

It seems that analysts are consistently underestimating HRB’s performance, but even that did not help the company’s share price over the last year, as it is down around 50%.

I expect the company to beat on both the top and bottom lines because the company is entering the seasonally important quarter with a favorable setup. With the company just posting a Q2 revenue beat and reaffirming its full-year guidance, I am expecting a surprise to the upside.

What I’m looking for

The company’s Wave segment has been showing consistently decent performance over the last while. This small-business financial software helps HRB diversify its revenue streams, and I am expecting to see decent performance going forward. Last quarter, Wave grew around 12%. But let’s be honest, no one is going to pay attention to the smaller revenue segments in Q3 because they are not the ones to be bringing in all the dough this time around. Sure, the smaller segments are great when the company is just surviving the boring seasons of no activity. The day-to-day of Q1 and Q2. All eyes will be on the company’s assisted tax preparation, US royalties, US DIY tax preparation, and Refund transfers. These segments come back to life, but no other segment compares to the assisted tax preparation. In Q3 ’25, this segment grew over 6.5%. I think that is quite a decent growth number, so anything below that, I would think there are some growth concerns and would need to get to hear the comments from the management on why that was the case.

Also, I would like to hear some comments regarding the progress of its Horizons transformation plan that was introduced all the way back in 2020. It’s been around 6 years since then, and yes, there has been some activity and push to become a bit more than a seasonal tax preparer, but the new segments are still very low compared to the juggernaut that is the assisted tax prep. I would like to hear what other acquisitions the company is looking to make, to expand its reach, and to make the boring non-tax seasons look a lot better.

It also seems like the company’s share price got hit by the fear of AI disruption. I am having a hard time believing that the company’s valuation was cut in half because of its fundamentals. Earnings will continue to grow at least double what its top-line growth will be, and that is a sign of a very healthy situation. I don’t ever expect anything in terms of top-line performance, but as long as the company becomes more profitable and efficient, I will be the first one to buy such a company, even if it seems boring.

The real reason for such a devastating blow to its valuation is the fear of AI and how AI is going to change how tax preparation workflows are managed. It seems that the Street thinks HRB will be left behind if it doesn’t adapt to the new technology. I agree with that sentiment to an extent; however, I don’t see how autonomous AI is going to replace humans in such important work, with so many regulations, and real repercussions if something is not filed correctly. There is no way I would rather let some AI agent do my tax returns, no matter how much I hate doing such paperwork. The technology, although impressive, cannot handle such big tasks without some sort of hallucination. I use AI agents essentially every day, and there is not a single time that I take its word at face value. So, the whole SaaSpocalypse, I think, was a massive overreaction, at least right now, and that means there were a lot of companies like HRB that got hit hard even though the fundamental picture is intact.

Of course, HRB is not going to fall victim to the ever-changing dynamic of technology today. It already offers AI Tax Assist, which is designed to make DIY filing easier by giving 24/7 tax help. Its AI was trained on tax scenarios, tested by many experts, and built around client trust. Augmentation is what I am looking for when it comes to tax filings, not replacement. I’d like to hear more such initiatives from the management.

Is it a good time to buy HRB before earnings?

I like to look at a valuation model to decide whether it is or not. Below are the assumptions I used.

I think the company is very easy to model, since I don’t expect any sort of surprises in terms of performance, whether that is going to be on the top line or profitability. Here, I went with 3% CAGR, as that is what analysts are assuming as well (more or less). For margins, 21.5% is what gives us the $5 of EPS for the next year, and then I just gradually increased it by just a little bit. Analysts see double-digit growth in earnings, but I am being more conservative here. The rest I kept as close to the company’s historical averages as I could. Also, for my discount rate, I went with 10%, as is standard for me, instead of the company’s low estimate of around 6%. That way, I am being even more conservative, as that provides quite a decent margin of safety. Also, I lowered my terminal growth rate to 1.5% instead of my usual 2.5%. So, with these estimates, we get the following projections.

As you can see, nothing fancy, just a solid mid-single-digit growth. Very achievable in the long run. And with these projections, we see that HRB’s intrinsic value is around $56.62 per share, which means the company is trading at a very attractive discount to its fair value.

Risks and Closing Comments

The AI fears will continue to apply pressure on the company’s share, as the sentiment continues to worsen, undeservedly so, might I add. The macroeconomic uncertainty will most likely play a huge role in the US economy in the future. The current administration is too unpredictable at times, which introduces an extra layer of volatility. The company might never recover because it will be seen as a legacy tax service provider, and the competition that may be more advanced and efficient will take its lunch. That is a possibility, but a far-fetched one in my opinion.

I think if you have already been holding HRB in your portfolio, I don’t see why you wouldn’t pick up some more shares at this price. If you bought it at a much higher price than now, I think you should like it a lot more now, especially if you believe that the whole AI disruption narrative is overdone, as I do. Therefore, I believe at these prices, the risk-to-reward is quite appealing, so I am upgrading the company to a buy.

data.title: Dutch Bros: De-Risking Ahead Of Earnings As Competition Heats Up (Downgrade)
data.description:
We're at an incredibly volatile juncture in the stock markets. Despite an unclear resolution to the conflict in Iran, plus a choppy macroeconomy with weak consumer spending, the S&P 500 has shot back up near all-time highs, reversing all of its earlier losses. Software stocks and consumer-oriented retail and restaurant names continue to see large downsides, while semiconductor and energy shares reap the majority of this year's gains. Stock selection is crucial, especially as we move into a potentially volatile Q1 earnings season.

Dutch Bros (BROS), in my view, will see a bit of vulnerability with its recent rebound rally. The stock is down ~15% since the start of the year, but so far investors have been generally pleased with its growth performance. The question now is, can Dutch Bros keep on top of consensus expectations, as the competitive winds may be shifting against the company's favor?

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Dutch Bros in March, when the stock was trading in the mid-$40s. In the space of a month, Dutch Bros has managed an impressive ~10% rebound rally. At the same time, I think there are a number of factors that have moved in the unfavorable direction for this stock, prompting me to cut my rating on the stock to "Neutral."

The next major catalyst for Dutch Bros is its Q1 earnings release, due on May 6. In my view, the stock is ripe for profit-taking ahead of that date.

All eyes on McDonald's as it steps up its drinks game

Since inception, the discussion around Dutch Bros has always been centered around how its comp sales trends are outpacing the major giant in the coffee industry: Starbucks (SBUX). But as Starbucks struggles and focuses on retaining its coffeehouse vibe through new CEO Brian Niccol's "Back to Starbucks" plan, increasingly it looks like Dutch Bros' real competition is McDonald's.

McDonald's has been an unbelievably bright spot in an inflation-pressured restaurant sector. Whereas most large restaurant chains have issues decelerating comp sales trends, McDonald's comp sales (which measure the growth from existing restaurant locations, excluding the contribution from newly opened stores) recently accelerated to 6.8% y/y in Q4, accelerating 440bps versus just 2.4% growth in Q3.

The company has credited its comps turnaround in the United States to its focus on value menu offerings, particularly its nationally advertised $5 Meal Deal.

Recently, McDonald's made a major announcement that shifts its major focus to drinks. The company is releasing a new lineup of energy drinks and custom sodas, and it's planning specifically on pricing these products at a low price to undercut Starbucks (SBUX) and Dutch Bros, per a report from the Wall Street Journal. McDonald's core aim is to bring in new customers outside core mealtimes and improve the slower day parts.

Dutch Bros' success in recent quarters hasn't just been due to coffee but also due to customers driving in for energy drinks in the middle of the day.

Already, on Dutch Bros' recent Q4 earnings call, the company was pushed on McDonald's running energy drink promotions in test markets. Dutch Bros CEO Christine Barone responded by minimizing the notion of competition, noting that Dutch Bros is one of the core innovators in the space.

So on the first question on the energy test, as we shared last quarter, we really didn't see anything in our business. I think we are the category creator of customized energy. And I think anything that's being shared about the category that creates new customers and new customer interest, I would guess that we would likely benefit from that. And so we do feel really confident about what we're doing from an energy perspective. We've been in the energy business for a very long time and really know what customers want in this space.

However, we think that McDonald's deeper entry into the energy drink category in Q2, and at deeper price cuts, will represent a fresh competitive threat for Dutch Bros. We note as well that one of Dutch Bros' core advantages versus Starbucks is the convenience of its drive-through lane: an advantage it doesn't have over McDonald's.

Q1 look-ahead

As previously mentioned, Dutch Bros is due to report Q1 earnings on May 6. I believe this will be a negative catalyst for the company. The key items to watch out for are:

  • Decelerating comp sales, owing in part to the run-up in gas prices. As a reminder, Dutch Bros has guided to 3-5% systemwide comp sales in FY26, versus 5.6% in FY25 as a whole and 7.7% in Q4. Already, Dutch Bros faces a tough comp in FY25, especially as it laps pricing increases. Even before we consider the impact of McDonald's (which will be more of a Q2 issue), we think there is likely to be under-recognized impact from soaring gas prices. Not only is this cutting into consumers' discretionary budgets, but many people may be pulling back on the frequency of their driving overall. For a company like Dutch Bros that relies so heavily on drive-through for its business, we don't expect that skyrocketing gas prices are good news.
  • Weakening shop contribution margins. Already as Dutch Bros was exiting Q4, the company was facing a slight ~1-point y/y decline in shop contribution profit margins to 27.6%. The company attributed this to a rise in coffee costs, and we don't expect much respite as we continue to see reports on elevated green coffee prices.
  • Comments around pricing power and competition. We do think there is a large chance that Dutch Bros will acknowledge the stiffer competition from peers like McDonald's in Q1, and be forced to run more frequent and deeper promotions to maintain market share among value-oriented consumers. This will have the impact of blunting average ticket and comp sales trends.

Valuation and key takeaways

Lastly, we also think Dutch Bros' recent rebound rally from February lows in the mid-$40s leaves the stock ripe for profit taking. Trading at 21x forward adjusted EBITDA, the company is trading much richer than McDonald's as well as direct rival Black Rock Coffee Bar, which is achieving stronger comp sales growth versus Dutch Bros.

We think there's a high likelihood of Dutch Bros' multiple re-rating back down as the competition narrative heats up and comp sales fade. Take the opportunity before earnings to de-risk your position in this stock.

data.title: GAM: Great Portfolio, But You're Not Getting The Discount Back
data.description:
General American Investors Company Inc. (GAM) has been around for nearly 100 years, and that makes it one of the oldest surviving CEFs, along with Adams Diversified Equity Fund, Inc. (ADX). Internally managed and built for long-term capital appreciation, GAM has proved its dependability, having survived the Great Depression, two world wars, multiple other wars, the oil crash of 1973, and so on. It has managed this by keeping things simple. There is no income engineering, no complicated strategies – what you see is what you earn.

On the surface, it looks like just the kind of compounding instrument long-term investors seek.

But then that fails to explain the persistent double-digit NAV discount the fund seems to perpetually trade at. Unlike peers with managed distribution policies, GAM does not offer a steady yield. Some years, it is terrific – other years, it is terrible. This gives the market no reason to completely close the discount gap – because in the CEF world, yield dependability is golden, and GAM doesn’t have it.

Here’s a chart of GAM’s dividend history for the last 3+ decades. You can see how variable the dividend is from year to year. This shows how GAM relies on realized gains and not income engineering to generate its dividend. In good years, we have massive distributions; in bad years, we don’t.

So should we buy GAM because of its discounted high-quality portfolio or avoid it because the discount never closes?

GAM Overview

General American Investors is a closed-end equity fund designed not for regular income but for long-term capital appreciation. For what GAM does – large cap, low turnover, long-term, no engineering – its 1.07% expense ratio is high.

The portfolio is relatively concentrated: Information Technology at 27.1%, Financials at 17.5%, and Industrials at 14.3%. Top holdings include Alphabet Inc. (GOOG), Microsoft Corporation, Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.B) and The TJX Companies, Inc. (TJX) at between 5% and 6% each. This is not a trading vehicle, no derivative usage, low leverage, no particular income strategy – it is more like a permanent capital structure for equity investing. Gains are allowed to compound, not systemically distributed.

GAM pays a small regular dividend and occasional special distributions when they have realized gains. Shareholder returns are derived from NAV growth – however, they only see additional realized returns when the persistent discount narrows over time.

Thus, GAM invests like an equity fund but trades at persistent discounts like a closed-end fund. This doesn’t especially create an opportunity because the discount never closes, although it does narrow sometimes. Unlike ETFs, there is no arbitrage mechanism to force price and NAV to converge.

The Investment Case For GAM

GAM takes a disciplined, long-term approach and allows winners to continue running. Management is not overactive. They don’t overtrade or sell positions too early. If you look at longer periods of time, this staid approach has worked well, compounding NAV strongly.

GAM’s lack of an artificial distribution policy also lets it avoid NAV erosion. GAM distributes capital only when it is actually generated. If underlying earnings are weak at any particular period, GAM does not sell stocks or use your own capital to support a fixed payout. Rather, in GAM’s case, in a bad year, shareholders simply get a lower dividend.

This makes GAM cleaner. It doesn’t need to sustain a yield, so it doesn’t need to realize gains when the investment is not mature. Nor is there hidden NAV erosion. Over time, this allows the fund to behave much more like a traditional long-term investment account than a yield product.

One benefit of buying GAM instead of replicating the underlying (besides the complexity of doing so) is the discipline it enforces. Retail investors tend to sell early, on early signs of positive returns, rotate too much, and mistime entries. GAM is basically a managed portfolio that enforces discipline.

From a forward-looking perspective, my expectations for GAM in 2026 are relatively straightforward. If equity markets deliver mid- to high-single-digit returns, GAM’s NAV should be broadly tracking that performance. I would expect meaningful special distributions if markets are stronger and realized gains are higher. In weaker markets, payouts may fall sharply. Given GAM’s broad turnover rate of around 20%, I don’t expect anything to change here. The discount may narrow from the current ~11% to closer to 8-9% if the markets are stronger. For GAM’s specific portfolio, I believe it is ultimately more sensitive to AI CapEx, data center scenarios, and rates rather than oil and geopolitics. If these things hold or strengthen, expect GAM to do well with its distribution this year.

Finally, the persistent discount itself can be viewed as a benefit. Investors are able to buy high-quality stocks at 10-15% below their underlying values. So, when you are buying at a discount, you get more of the underlying asset for each dollar invested – literally “more bang for the buck.”

Whether the discount closes or not is immaterial in this argument because this, by itself, gives shareholders an embedded margin of safety. If the discount closes, they get enhanced returns; if it doesn’t, they have that theoretical margin. The idea is to look at GAM’s historical discount levels and buy it at the highest possible discount.

Over the last five years, GAM’s discount has ranged from roughly ~-20% to ~-8%. Long-term average is around -15%. That means today’s ~-10.8% discount is below the long-term average.

The Investment Case Against GAM

That persistent discount can also be viewed as a problem. While it sounds attractive in theory, in practice, though, GAM has spent decades trading below its intrinsic value. The discount does widen and narrow, but never really closes. There are no clear catalysts that say this will change. So, investors are left with a high-quality portfolio that they bought for cheap but that the market simply refuses to fully recognize.

A related problem is that GAM has no real demand driver. There is no managed distribution or stable yield profile, so income investors may not find it appealing enough. Since a fixed yield is not supported, new capital doesn’t flow in to close the discount even when the performance is strong. Trading volume, consequently, is low compared to many peers.

The dividend itself creates another limitation. Some years, there are large distributions that can make the overall yield appear attractive. In some years, in weaker markets, distributions can fall sharply. For investors seeking consistency or planning around income, this makes GAM an unreliable vehicle.

Finally, I have noted that GAM being internally managed with low turnover is a strength. However, that can also create inertia. Since there is no fixed payout, management doesn’t have any pressure to optimize the structure. They simply distribute what they earn, mostly through realized gains. That unchanging structure is a strength for some investors and a weakness for others.

A Useful Contrast: GAM vs. ADX

A useful way to understand GAM is to compare it with Adams Diversified Equity Fund. I covered ADX in February. GAM and ADX have some similarities and one major difference. The similarities are that both are internally managed, long-duration CEFs; both have low turnover and focus on equity investing. Both have delivered strong NAV performance over the years.

The difference – this is the core – is that ADX follows a managed distribution policy.

ADX targets an annual payout of roughly 6-8% of NAV, regardless of whether those returns come from income or realized gains. This gives it a consistent yield profile attractive to investors looking for consistent income. I noted that this is true even when the underlying economics are not clear. In weaker markets, this can, in theory, cause NAV erosion.

Given its engineered yield, ADX tends to attract retail income investors more. That volume supports its market price, sometimes even meaningfully narrowing the discount. When that happens, shareholders benefited, which I noted in my earlier article was a key reason I preferred buying ADX at discounted prices.

GAM, without a consistent yield, remains largely outside the income-focused CEF universe. So its discount is wider and more persistent and less prone to regular re-rating. This isn’t necessarily a flaw, but it is a critical difference.

Here’s how I will compare the two funds:

Conclusion: Who Should Buy

GAM has a strong asset base of high-quality, large-cap stocks. It has compounded capital effectively over the years – at some periods, even better than the S&P 500. However, the discounted structure ensures that the quality never gets reflected in the market price. That discount is not an inefficiency waiting to close; therefore, it is not a defined opportunity but a structural feature that can sometimes narrow and benefit investors. Sometimes, it doesn’t.

That description says it all about who GAM is suitable for. GAM is a simple and very basic way to access long-term equity compounding without going through the trouble of buying them all yourself. GAM works best if you are holding for a very long term, if you prioritize NAV growth over a consistent income.

data.title: BioAge Labs: Oral NLRP3 Inhibitor Could Establish PoC In CV This Year
data.description:
Investment Overview

This is my first time providing coverage of BioAge Labs (BIOA), a biotech company based in Emeryville, California, that completed its IPO in October 2024, raising ~$238m via the issuance of 13.23m shares priced at $18 per share.

BioAge's lead product candidate is BGE-102, indicated for cardiovascular disease and ophthalmology indications, which it describes as follows (source: 2025 annual report/10K filing):

a potent, structurally novel, orally available, brain-penetrant small-molecule NLRP3 inhibitor. BGE-102 has a distinct mechanism and binding site from other NLRP3 inhibitors in development with issued patents covering both composition of matter and claims for the unique binding site.

BioAge's start to life as a listed company was somewhat disastrous. In December 2024, the company reported it was discontinuing a Phase 2 study for its weight-loss drug azelaprag over safety concerns - at the time, azelaprag was its lead clinical candidate.

BioAge revealed that "liver transaminitis without clinically significant symptoms was observed in some subjects receiving azelaprag," and concluded that "the emerging safety profile of the current doses tested is not consistent with our goal of a best-in-class oral obesity therapy."

Azelaprag was being tested in combo with Eli Lilly's tirzepatide - better known as the obesity drug Zepbound or Type 2 Diabetes ("T2D") drug Mounjaro, with combined 2025 net sales of >$35bn - as a way to potentially help patients preserve lean muscle mass when on tirzepatide. News of the discontinuation saw BioAge stock crater from $20 per share to <$4.5 per share.

Just over a year later, however, in January of this year, stock was back trading at ~$20 per share, the gains being driven by the progress of BGE-102. NLR proteins are involved in the immune system, helping to start and regulate the immune system's response to injury, toxins, or foreign invaders.

BioAge says that "chronic NLRP3 activity drives disease & predicts poor human longevity," and adds (in its 10K filing) that

Our first therapeutic area for BGE-102 is cardiometabolic disease, with a focus on atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD) risk reduction. Chronic systemic inflammation, as measured by high-sensitivity C-reactive protein (hsCRP), is an independent risk factor for cardiovascular events that is not adequately addressed by current lipid-lowering and antihypertensive therapies.

BioAge Stock Spikes On Promising Phase 1 Data

On 4th December last year, BioAge announced positive interim Phase 1 data for BGE-102, noting that:

  • BGE-102 was well-tolerated in SAD (single ascending dose) and initial MAD (multiple ascending dose) cohorts, with a pharmacokinetic profile supporting once-daily oral dosing
  • Strong target engagement: BGE-102 achieved 90-98% suppression of IL-1β, a cytokine directly downstream of NLRP3, at Day 14
  • High brain penetration: BGE-102 doses of 60 mg and higher exceeded target IC90 levels in cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) at Day 14

On 12th Jan this year, the company shared more positive data from the same study, showing "rapid and profound reduction in inflammatory markers." Specifically:

  • BGE-102 achieved 86% reduction in hsCRP at Day 14, with 93% of participants reaching normalized levels (<2 mg/L)
  • BGE-102 demonstrated significant reductions in IL-6, a key driver of systemic inflammation and cardiovascular risk, and fibrinogen, an independent predictor of cardiovascular events
  • BGE-102 was well tolerated with a favorable safety profile

hsCRP stands for high-sensitivity C-reactive protein test - according to the Mayo Clinic website:

A high level of hs-CRP in the blood has been linked to an increased risk of heart attacks. Also, people who have had a heart attack are more likely to have another heart attack if they have a high hs-CRP level. But their risk goes down when their hs-CRP level is in the typical range.

The encouraging, albeit early-stage, data has led BioAge to conclude that BGE-102 may be able to treat conditions such as neuroinflammation, given its ability to move between the blood/brain barrier and peripheral inflammation, for example, in cardiovascular disease or metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis ("MASH"), which are both double-digit billion-dollar markets.

Paul Rubin, MD, Chief Medical Officer of BioAge, stated in a press release that:

These data provide strong rationale for advancing into our planned Phase 2a study, where we will evaluate BGE-102's effects on a range of key inflammatory biomarkers over a longer duration in patients with elevated cardiovascular risk.

The company subsequently completed a share offering, raising ~$132m, at ~$19.5 per share. Announcing its full-year 2025 earnings last month, BioAge stated that it:

estimates that the proceeds from this financing, together with our $285.1 million in cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities as of December 31, 2025, will be sufficient to fund operations through 2029 based on its current operating plan.

Net loss for 2025 was reported as $(80.6m), having spent ~$73.9m on R&D for the year, with G&A expenses reaching ~$27.8m.

The company has also highlighted upcoming milestones as follows:

  • 1H 2026: Completion of Phase 1 trial with full data readout, including two additional MAD cohorts in obese participants with elevated hsCRP
  • 1H 2026: Initiation of Phase 2a proof-of-concept study in patients with obesity and cardiovascular risk factors. The trial is planned to enroll approximately 100 patients randomized 1:1 to BGE-102 monotherapy or placebo for 12 weeks. The anticipated primary endpoint is percent change in hsCRP. The trial will also assess inflammatory and metabolic biomarkers, and will include liver MRI.
  • 2H 2026: Phase 2a data readout

Regarding ophthalmology indications, the company says it is planning an initial proof-of-concept study planned in patients with diabetic macular edema ("DME"), which is expected to read out data in "mid-2027."

Ongoing Opportunity In Obesity & Big Pharma Partners

We may also note that, while BGE-102 may not be weight loss focused, the company continues to work on developing a drug to target the apelin-APJ pathway - which may modulate cardiac contractility and vascular tone and was the target of its original lead candidate azelaprag - hoping to submit an investigational new drug ("IND") application to the FDA this year in order to enter the clinic with a new candidate. The company is advancing this candidate in collaboration with China-based Pharma JiKang Therapeutics.

Given that in 2025 the cardiovascular/obesity-focused company Metsera was acquired by Pfizer (PFE) for $7bn, potentially rising to $10bn - after a bidding war with Novo Nordisk (NVO) - and Novo itself struck a $2.2bn deal with Septerna (SEPN) to develop obesity candidates last year, this could be an asset worth watching. Pharma giants Merck (MRK) and AstraZeneca (AZN) are also developing obesity drugs in collaboration with China-based biotech companies.

Partnerships with both Eli Lilly (LLY) and Novartis (NVS), albeit not involving any milestone payments so far as I am aware, also offer more reasons for optimism that BioAge ought to be a stock worth monitoring in 2026.

The Novartis deal is apparently "focused on discovering novel therapeutic targets at the intersection of aging biology and exercise physiology," while alongside Lilly ExploR&D, it is focused on "development of therapeutic antibodies targeting novel metabolic aging targets identified through BioAge's discovery platform."

Analysis - Assessing The Investment Opportunity In Relation To BioAge

BioAge's candidate selection is apparently based on research conducted by the company using its technology platform, which the company says in its 10K filing "provides unique insight into human aging biology spanning over 50 years."

Re BGE102, the company also states:

We found that increased transcription of genes for NLRP3, IL-1ß, and IL-18 in our human aging cohorts was associated with significantly increased all-cause mortality risk.

These findings align with human genetic evidence from Mendelian randomization studies, which demonstrate that a one standard deviation increase in NLRP3 expression is associated with up to a 70% increase in heart failure risk. Additionally, gain-of-function mutations in NLRP3 have been associated with reduced lean mass, impaired body composition, and accelerated atherosclerosis.

Through a collaboration with Dr. Matthias Geyer at the University of Bonn, we identified a binding site on NLRP3 that is distinct from previously described inhibitors, including the reference compound MCC950, as confirmed by cryo-electron microscopy.

Unlike MCC950 and related inhibitors, which have been reported to inhibit NLRP3 ATPase activity, BGE-102 has not demonstrated ATPase inhibition in our in vitro assays, suggesting a fundamentally distinct mechanism that may offer advantages in specificity or side effect profile.

There is no shortage of competition in the NLRP3 space, although no NLRP3 drugs have secured commercial approval to date - BioAge cites Eli Lilly (LLY), Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY), Merck (MRK), Novo Nordisk (NVO), AstraZeneca (AZN), Neumora (NMRA), and a number of other private biotechs as rival NLRP3 drug developers. In the APJ agonist space, Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY), Structure Therapeutics (GPCR), and Sanofi (SNY) are all apparently developing similar assets.

While some of BioAge's early data intrigues - the hsCRP reduction of 86% in obese patients is described as potentially "best-in-class" by the company - the acid tests of efficacy and safety are still to come.

Management has pitched its lead candidate as being an oral version of an IL-6 inhibitor - 4 IL-6 inhibiting drugs have been FDA approved - ocilizumab (Actemra), Sarilumab (Kevzara), Siltuximab (Sylvant), and Satralizumab (Enspryng). Roche's Actemra earned >$3bn revenues in 2025 in the indication of rheumatoid arthritis ("RA") and others.

It's hard to see how the data releases due in the first half of this year will add much to the data already shared by the company, but the Phase 2a results due at the end of the year present an intriguing catalyst in my view.

The planned study will enroll 160 patients, divided into four cohorts - placebo, 30mg daily, 60mg daily, and 90mg daily, and will last for 16 weeks, with the treatment period ending after 12 weeks. The primary endpoint will be % change in hsCRP, with secondary endpoints covering CV risk factors.

As we can see above, there is no questioning the market opportunity for a drug that can reduce the risk of cardiovascular disease, and the oral availability of BGE102 is a potentially important competitive advantage, although we should note there are already a wide variety of heart disease drugs on the market, setting clinical study efficacy and safety bars high.

The fact that Eli Lilly paid ~$1.2bn to acquire Ventyx Pharma and its "portfolio of class-leading NLRP3 inhibitors" also highlights how highly prized the drug class is - BioAge's current market cap is $750m, so I would expect its valuation to increase if the Phase 2a results support the promise of the Phase 1 results.

Until then, BioAge must be considered a higher-risk investment, but with sufficient funding in place, two potentially promising drugs, one of which targets both CV risk and ophthalmology (another hugely lucrative but heavily congested space with approved and late-stage pipeline assets), two Big Pharma partners, and intriguing data catalysts due this year, I am willing to assign BioAge stock a Buy rating at this time, based on the opportunities in play offering more potential reward than risk of failure - but as a final caveat, I'd urge investors to remember what happened to candidate azelaprag.

data.title: IonQ: Q-Day Deep Dive
data.description:
Investment Thesis

While I am probably one of the biggest bulls out there, shares of quantum computing firm IonQ (IONQ) have dropped over 8% YTD on the back of a risk-off market that has punished companies that are mainly valued on their future potential (such as IonQ). As a bull, this has been frustrating.

While shares are clearly in a bear market compared to their all time high, I think the fundamental story has continued to improve for the quantum industry as a whole and for IonQ. Recent papers put out both by Google (GOOGL) and by researchers here in the US have shown that the number of estimated q-bits needed to hack traditional encryption techniques (such as the technology that secures Bitcoin) have dropped by over 90% in the last year.

In essence, the ‘doomsday’ for many cybersecurity analysts in the future where hackers can break traditional encryption and steal financial data, money itself, or do something even worse, is far closer than people originally expected.

The result is really a game changer for IonQ. I believe this will accelerate the timeline many CISOs follow in making their networks ‘quantum ready.’ For IonQ, this is a huge accelerant for their Quantum Key Distribution business.

IonQ could be on the precipice of their own ‘ChatGPT’ moment where quantum cyber becomes just as real as AI has become over the last four years.

With this, shares continue to be a Strong Buy despite the sell off.

Why I’m Doing Follow Up Coverage

While IonQ shares have had a big move this week on the back of world quantum day and their new DARPA contract, they are still down roughly 10% from the last time I wrote on them before Q4 earnings (back in January). I think this drop is unwarranted.

IonQ’s story over the last two and a half months has improved significantly. The market is really starting to realize the need for making global IT networks post-quantum secure.

With this, I believe the company is now facing its ‘ChatGPT’ moment, where the market suddenly realizes the value their post quantum technology can bring to both commercial and government clients. Management at IonQ agrees with me.

The purpose of this follow up coverage is to show that the market has shifted rapidly over the last 2-3 months. There are years when nothing happens in quantum. Fortunately for investors, there have been years worth of developments in just the last three months.

Q-Day Deep Dive

To understand what Q-Day is, we have to understand how modern web encryption technology works.

Most of the modern web/digital traffic relies on a RSA to AES-256 encryption key handoff.

How this works is that modern internet traffic shares an initial key to establish a secure connection between two end points using RSA (Rivest-Shamir-Adleman) based encryption. This allows for two end points to establish a secure connection. After this, AES-256 based encryption (which is lightweight and efficient) is used for every subsequent transmission of data from two end points until the connection between two end points is severed.

The benefit of AES-256 encryption is that it is quantum resistant already. The problem with modern encryption is that RSA based encryption is not quantum secure.

In fact, new research shows that RSA based encryption could be broken by quantum computers in as little as five years with the number of Q-bits needed by quantum computers just a fraction of what researchers estimated they would need just a year ago.

The effects of this could be catastrophic to any company that doesn’t upgrade their IT networks to being post quantum soon enough. Even if companies wait 2-3 years to upgrade their tech (on the assumption that they have five years), companies could still suffer from a harvest now and decrypt later attack, where hackers store copies of your data now and wait till technology is advanced enough to break RSA encryption.

Add into the mix Anthropic’s new Mythos model, which is a serious enough cyber threat that the Treasury secretary called an emergency meeting with major banks over it, and cybersecurity is about to face a once in a generation emergency set of upgrades.

IonQ is right at the center of it. As I mentioned in my last piece of research on IonQ:

Quantum key distribution based cybersecurity is unhackable because it is based on core principles in physics.

The core of cybersecurity in the future will be post quantum tech. I believe Quantum Key Distribution will be the center of this. I will explain this more in the Risks section, but IonQ is a leader here on many fronts for post quantum cryptography.

Valuation

On the surface, a traditional value investor would see IonQ as overvalued. On a forward price to sales basis, Seeking Alpha data shows the company trades at 55.79 times forward sales.

This would be 1,700% higher than the sector median.

But, what Seeking Alpha data does not take into account is the pending SkyWater merger. This merger is projected to bring IonQ’s combined forward revenues to roughly $1 billion. At this run rate (and growing), shares would be at 13 times forward sales based on today's market cap of $13 billion.

Given this, I think shares are cheap on a historical valuation basis. IonQ’s growth inflection is just starting, not slowing. Quantum computing attacks from Q-Day could cost the US economy over $3.3 trillion dollars annually.

In essence, there is $3.3 trillion of value destruction that is possible on an annual basis (since this is calculated off of GDP which is an annual figure). I believe this makes the post quantum cryptography market worth a solid 10% of this over the next five years, or $330 billion.

IonQ is arguably best positioned to grab this market given their QKD technology and their quantum randomness generators (more on both below). I think there is a real world where the company is doing $5 billion in revenue in five years (up from a projected $1 billion combined this year). Forward Wall Street estimates project $1.21 billion in non SkyWater revenues alone by 2030. I think $5 billion in combined revenues coupled with post quantum security could get the company here.

Given this, I think shares should trade at 20 times forward combined sales, generating a market cap of $20 billion. If this came true, this would represent 53% upside in shares from here.

Not a bad return for investing in a company that could help save the world from Q-Day.

Risks

I really believe IonQ is building one of the most complete quantum computing ecosystems with their acquisitions over the last 12 months. Their crown-jewel buyout of SkyWater which could become the TSMC of quantum computing semiconductors.

Despite this, the company’s biggest risk may actually come from the US government itself.

I wrote about this back in January but the US government (specifically the Department of War) has banned the purchase of Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) technology, or critical hardware used to make warfighter technology mathematically secure from quantum surface.

On the surface, this sounds completely backwards. I actually think this is one of IonQ’s biggest moats.

As the US government moves to ban the purchase of QKD technology, I expect the company to be able to lean into one of its other strong-suits: quantum random number generation (QRNG).

For those who don’t know how random number generation currently works in a software program, software random number generation is not truly ‘random.’ Software that is in charge of random number generation cycles through an extremely large list of numbers to spit out random numbers that become the foundation of modern software cryptography.

The issue with this method is obvious (especially with quantum computers): it's not actually random.

So, with a large enough machine learning model that can pattern match one of these strings of numbers for cryptography, traditional encryption algorithms break because the keys for a secure communication (data in flight) tend to only be switched once per new communication session between two end points. After all, if they were switched faster, the defined list of ‘random’ numbers to seed the keys would run out sooner.

This is where QRNG comes in. QRNG allows for encryption software (or hardware) to harness true randomness so the data it's encrypting is truly secure. The added benefit of this is that encryption keys can be cycled faster since randomness can be harnessed more frequently.

While IonQ is not the only company that offers QRNGs, they are one of the few companies that can sell QRNGs in a DOW compliant manner.

As part of this key memo from last year, the DOW banned the purchase of QRNG services via remote server access. In essence, this meant DOW software could not ping a third party QRNG service for their random number and then use it for encryption (because the random number could be potentially visible in transit).

For IonQ (via its acquisition of ID Quantique) the company is able to offer locally deployed QRNGs. So while this is not a complete QKD solution, this is a serious foothold the company has in whatever plans the DOW has for modernizing its cybersecurity.

Bottom Line

While Q-Day sounds like something out of a sci-fi novel or a dystopian future, the reality is that it could be less than five years away.

I believe that globally this will represent one of the biggest changes to tech infrastructure since the invention of the internet itself. I think this will be on the same order as the Y2K mania that forced thousands of companies to upgrade their servers overnight in order to get ready.

Quantum computing will inevitably break classical encryption. And as we get closer to it, IonQ has a once in a generation opportunity to go after the $2 Trillion cybersecurity market with a series of hardware products that will be ultra competitive compared to current cyber security solutions.

The problem with most modern cyber solutions is that they are software based and don’t stop man in the middle attacks that quantum computers (via Shor’s Algorithm) will be exceptional at. IonQ has multiple solutions to help.

As Peter Thiel has been famously quoted saying “the world is moving back from the bit and to the atom,” I think the world of cyber is doing the same. Away from complex software solutions that don’t address Q-Day, and back to a focus on hardware technology that was built from the ground up to fight quantum attacks.

With this, I believe IonQ shares remain a strong buy.

data.title: Biohaven: Downgrade To Hold On Troriluzole Setback And Obesity Data 2nd Half 2026
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Biohaven Ltd. (BHVN) it was with a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Biohaven: Despite Troriluzole Program Delay, FDA Review On Track For Q4 2025." With respect to this article, I mentioned that the company was approaching its review by the FDA of its drug troriluzole for the treatment of patients with Spinocerebellar Ataxia [SCA]. I had a Strong Buy on this stock the last time around based on this key event.

I had hoped that comparing the data it obtained with troriluzole in the 206-RWE study to that of external controls with respect to the primary and secondary endpoints would be enough to warrant U.S. marketing approval. Unfortunately, this was not the case, as the FDA came back for this program with a Complete Response Letter [CRL] of troriluzole. Due to this event happening, I'm downgrading this stock from a Strong Buy to a Hold rating.

The reason why is not only was this CRL received, but this program remains in limbo. Why do I say that? First and foremost, the CRL noted that the company must meet with a division of the FDA to discuss the potential of being able to see what kind of evidence might be needed to file a future New Drug Application [NDA] submission of troriluzole for the treatment of these patients with SCA. Secondly, the latest update for it is that the company held a Type A meeting with the FDA to initiate an appeal process. The company remains committed to working with the FDA, but since there is no path laid out at the moment, that is the reason for my downgrade to a Hold rating.

The second reason is because the company had enforced a restructuring of its business, with the key item being having to focus its resources on specific pipeline products. Not only that, but it was mentioned in November of 2025 that it had to reduce its annual direct R&D spend by 60%. All is not lost, though, as it has shifted its focus to three key late-stage assets.

The first incorporates a host of candidates under the development of Molecular Degrader of Extracellular Proteins [MODE] and Targeted Removal of Aberrant Protein [TRAP] extracellular protein degradation. Specifically, BHV-1400 for the targeting of IgA Nephropathy [IgAN] and BHV-1300 for the targeting of patients with Graves' disease. The second key development item would be that of Kv7 ion channel activator Opakalim for the treatment of patients with focal epilepsy.

The third key program to focus on is the one I'm going to go over in detail below, which is the development of the myostatin-activin pathway inhibitor taldefgrobep alfa being explored in a phase 2 proof-of-concept study for the treatment of patients with obesity. With the CRL attained for troriluzole for the treatment of patients with SCA, plus the continued advancement of three key programs still going forward, I believe that this stock should be downgraded to a Hold rating.

Troriluzole Program is Up in Air, but Company Still Has a Pipeline for Backup Options

As I mentioned above, Biohaven received a CRL for its drug troriluzole for the treatment of patients with SCA. The current progress, from what I see in the 10-K SEC Filing, page 23, is that the company held a Type A meeting to initiate an appeal process for this program. It is still committed to finding a path forward for it in terms of an NDA filing, but there is no assurance that it will be able to do so.

Despite this problem, the company has chosen to focus on three key programs, which I mentioned above. One of them is going to be an important one because it is going after the very large obesity market. This would be the evaluation of myostatin-activin pathway inhibitor [MAPI] taldefgrobep alfa for the treatment of patients with obesity in a phase 2 proof of concept [POC] study. The notion here is that this drug offers a novel mechanism of action [MOA] by providing the ability not only for weight loss but also to increase lean mass [muscle mass]. Along with providing benefits in multiple metabolic parameters to help patients.

Before going over this specific mid-stage trial, plus any catalysts to come about from this program, I believe that it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity could be.

Obesity is characterized as a person having too much body fat. The thing is that while it is a cosmetic concern for sure, it is also more so a problem in terms of a person's health. The reason why is because obesity can cause a wide range of comorbidities, such as the following:

  • High cholesterol
  • High blood pressure
  • Type 2 Diabetes [T2D]
  • Heart problems
  • Liver disease.

In order for a person to be considered obese, they need to have a body mass index [BMI] score of 30 or higher. However, this isn't the only measure to be required, because athletes with muscles can also have a BMI ranging in that category. That's why it is also important to get other measurements, like a waist circumference. It's not an easy thing to conquer because there are many reasons that can lead to obesity. They could be medications that actually cause fat buildup, or there might be other factors like stress, environment, and/or reduced physical activity. It is a collection of items that can cause it. The global obesity treatment market size is projected to reach $60.53 billion by 2030. This is a very large market opportunity for sure, but as you might know, there are a lot of GLP-1 drugs out on the market right now.

The thing is that taldefgrobep alfa is not looking to become another GLP-1 drug. It is instead looking to change the scope of being able to treat obesity patients through providing a novel MOA for starters. Much more than that, it goes back to what I stated above, which is that it is looking to provide as much fat loss as possible but, at the same time, to increase muscle mass and bone density. Why is this crucial? That's because current GLP-1 drugs are effective at allowing for weight loss, but a major issue is that they result in significant muscle loss. That is, 40% of the weight loss for patients that occurs is that of lean mass [Biohaven Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #49].

The second possible advantage of taldefgrobep alfa is that it is intended to have reduced side effects. A major problem with GLP-1 drugs now would be the side effects, which are primarily gastrointestinal [GI]-related. It is hoping to counter this problem by having its myostatin-activin pathway inhibitor be given as once-monthly dosing through an easy-to-use autoinjector.

The MOA of taldefgrobep alfa is to bind to active myostatin on GDF-11 and form a complex to attach to ActRIIA/B and prevent ActRII binding. This is important because it accomplishes two key items. One is the prevention of recruitment of activin-like kinases ALK 4, 5, and 7 and prevents downstream phosphorylated Smad2/3 signaling, which is important to block. By accomplishing all of this, there are two effects that happen as direct effects on fat and muscle:

  • With respect to fat, fat cells have lipolysis occur, which is the breakdown of triglycerides to glycerol and free fatty acids to generate energy [reduces overall fat storage for the patient].
  • With respect to muscle mass, preventing downstream phosphorylated Smad2/3 signaling also results in increased muscle mass taking shape [increased muscle mass].

Myostatin-activin pathway inhibition is a proven class model. How so? Well, that's because there are many biotechs that are currently evaluating a drug targeting the Myostatin Activin Inhibitor Class [Biohaven Corporate Presentation, March 2026, slide #50]. Furthermore, the company points to a particular study with Eli Lilly (LLY) with activin receptor blocker bimagrumab as a monotherapy. Specifically, in the BELIEVE study, it showed that this drug was able to achieve comparable body fat mass reduction compared to high-dose semaglutide [2.4 mg] at 1 year. The end result being bigagrumab treated patients achieved a -25.3% reduction in weight loss, while those given semaglutide achieved a -24.8%.

Not only did this drug achieve this feat, but it was shown to have a greater reduction of visceral adipose tissue and improvement of lean muscle mass. The last one being important because patients who took Eli Lilly's drug achieved an improvement of lean muscle mass of +2.7%, whereas those on semaglutide actually saw a drop in such muscle mass of -7.9%.

What this data does is prove that this drug was definitely comparable to and/or beat out semaglutide on several measures. There was just one problem with this drug, which was the safety profile of it. Specifically, there were high rates of Diarrhea, muscle spasms, and acne.

Wait a second, how does Biohaven hope to overcome this specific issue with the Myostatin Activin Inhibitor Class? The reason why bimagrumab fell into trouble is because of irreversible ActRIIB binding. The thing is that taldefgrobep alfa has the complex that binds to ActRIIB. But the key point is that it allows other signaling [important signaling] to still happen. Thus, the way that this company has its complex bind to this allows it to avoid the irreversible ActRIIB binding and ultimately potentially achieve a better safety profile. This point is proven in the many patients treated with its drug. It has been able to avoid the issues of other Myostatin Activin Pathway Inhibitors as shown here in the presentation [Biohaven Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #52].

In order to see whether or not taldegrobep alfa is going to be able to effectively help patients with obesity, the company is currently in the process of running a phase 2 POC study. This randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, dose-ranging study is expected to recruit up to a total of 150 patients. These patients are to be randomized 2:2:1:1 as follows:

  • 50 patients given once-a-week [Q1W] taldefgrobep alfa.
  • 50 patients given once every 4 weeks [Q4W] taldefgrobep alfa.
  • 25 patients given Q1W blinded placebo.
  • 25 patients given Q4W blinded placebo.

The point here is that the Q1W patients will make up one portion of this study and move on to a 24-week OLE study. Then, the Q4W-treated patients will be another portion of this trial and then enter a 24-week OLE study of their own afterwards. The primary efficacy endpoint is going to be the measure of percentage of total body weight at Week 24. The main thing to note is that enrollment for this specific mid-stage trial has completed enrollment. With that said, it believes that it is on track to release data from this phase 2 POC study using taldefgrobep alfa to treat these obesity patients by the 2nd half of 2026.

Financials

According to the 10-K SEC Filing, Biohaven had cash, cash equivalents, marketable securities, and restricted cash of $322 million as of December 31, 2025. The reason for the cash on hand is because back on November 13, 2025, it enacted a public offering, whereby it was able to raise $188.7 million of net proceeds after expenses. In addition, it also obtained $250 million in April of 2025 from a sale of senior secured notes under a Note Purchase Agreement.

As I mentioned above, the company had to reduce its R&D spending after the problem it had with its advancement of troriluzole for the treatment of patients with SCA. Despite these financial transactions in place, it still didn't expect its cash on hand to fund its operations for an extended period of time. It believed that the cash on hand would only be enough to allow it to fund its operations for at least 1 year as of the date of the 10-K SEC filing, which was filed on March 2, 2026.

The thing is that this was not going to be enough cash, and thus it had to generate additional funds. Subsequent to the quarter ending December 31, 2025, it had to sell an additional 17.2 million common shares for net proceeds of $178.9 million. However, it had to use $42.7 million to pay Knopp Biosciences LLC. In my opinion, I believe that the company is going to have to raise additional cash going forward, possibly this year.

The company burned through $745.4 million of cash in 2025. This is broken down into $635.1 million in R&D expenses and then $110.3 million in G&A expenses.
