Risks to Business

There are several risks to consider before investing in Biohaven. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the CRL the company had received for troriluzole for the treatment of patients with SCA. The company held a Type A FDA meeting to start an appeal process. The company believes that it can interact with the FDA to obtain a path forward for an NDA of troriluzole to treat these patients.

The risk here is that there is no assurance that this appeal process laid out by the company will be successful in allowing them to refile an NDA submission of troriluzole for these SCA patients. Even if an NDA is eventually filed for this program, there is no guarantee that the company will be able to obtain U.S. marketing approval of this for them either.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the ongoing phase 2 POC study using taldefgrobep alfa for the treatment of patients with obesity. The company has a few things going for it with respect to this program. The first of which is that it has been shown to be safe/tolerable in >700 patients treated. In addition, Myostatin Activin Pathway Inhibition has been proven in several drug candidates already, including that of bimagrumab.

The risk here is that there is no guarantee that there won't be any safety issues arising in the ongoing phase 2 POC study or when actual data from it is released in the latter part of this year. Secondly, the goal of the company is to establish taldefgrobep alfa with bimagrumab-like efficacy while maintaining such a good safety profile. The risk here is that there is no assurance that the company is going to release positive data in the 2nd half of 2026 from this phase 2 POC trial, for starters. Nor is such weight loss data going to be better than that of bimagrumab or any other approved GLP-1 therapy on the market. If it fails to obtain equal or superior data to what is currently available for weight loss, then the stock price could suffer because of it.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of its selective Kv7 activator, Opakalim, which is being developed for the treatment of patients with focal epilepsy in two pivotal trials. The first of two of these pivotal trials is expected to have data released from it using this drug to treat these patients in the 2nd half of 2026. Preliminary data from an open-label extension [OLE] study in January of 2026 revealed that patients who took 75 mg of once-daily Opakalim for 6 months achieved a ≥50% reduction in seizure frequency compared to pretreatment baseline.

The risk here, first and foremost, is that there is no assurance that this data obtained from the OLE study is going to be replicated in either of these two late-stage trials. Secondly, the pivotal studies deploy a placebo in place. Thus, the risk here is that there is no assurance that Opakalim is going to be able to achieve statistically significant reductions in seizures compared to that of placebo. The company boasts a superior tolerability profile compared to other anti-seizure medications. The other risk is that upon release of data, there is no guarantee that the safety profile established thus far is going to hold up.

Conclusion

Biohaven has done well to restructure its company after what had happened with respect to the CRL of troriluzole for the treatment of patients with SCA. It was able to shift its focus to the three key program areas I mentioned in the beginning above. Specifically, the setback for the CRL of this drug is why I had decided it was best to downgrade this stock from a Strong Buy to a Hold rating.

However, I believe that there is potential based on its three key programs I mentioned, including that of its development of Myostatin-activin pathway inhibitor taldefgrobep alfa for the treatment of patients with obesity. With a data readout of this drug for the treatment of these patients in the ongoing phase 2 POC study in the 2nd half of 2026, plus the release of topline data from the pivotal study using Opakalim for patients with Focal Epilepsy, I believe that this stock deserves a Hold rating for now.

data.title: Morgan Stanley: Impressive Q1 But Valuation Remains Hot
data.description:
As shown in the chart below, I have changed my view on Morgan Stanley (MS) quite a few times in the past year or so. Most recently, in mid-January, I upgraded the stock from a sell to a hold as the fundamentals were rock solid. Why didn't I rate Morgan Stanley as a buy? Well, despite strong profitability and execution, the valuation appeared stretched. After my hold rating, the stock pulled back but has since rebounded. Earlier this morning (April 15th), the firm reported their 2026 Q1 earnings, and today I'll be providing an important update amid an uncertain macro and geopolitical backdrop.

In the below analysis, it is shown that Morgan Stanley's Q1 was robust. Growth was strong in their Institutional Securities and Wealth Management segments, and the firm's efficiency was another highlight. Profitability ratios like the ROE were impressive. Despite some risks, including private credit exposure and cybersecurity threats, the fundamentals were overall more than solid. However, with the P/B ratio still being very far above its historic medians, I believe that the excellent execution has already been fully priced in or more. Therefore, as much as I'm impressed with the firm, I have decided to just reiterate my hold rating for now.

Q1 Overview

Let's start today with a look at some of their company-wide metrics. For 2026 Q1, Morgan Stanley generated a total of $20.58 billion worth of revenues, up 16% YoY and beating expectations by around $820 million. This is a notable acceleration of growth from Q4's 10% increase, and so there are signs that overall business activity is accelerating despite the geopolitical environment having deteriorated. Of course, it will be shown below that their trading helped to power these strong top-line numbers. For provision for credit losses, Morgan Stanley saw a decrease YoY as a signal that the firm has confidence regarding the outlook despite how the backdrop looks now, and of course that is a good sign.

In terms of business efficiency and profitability, there are great signs for the firm. Firstly, their expense efficiency ratio retreated from 68% to 65% YoY as a demonstration that the business is getting increasingly well run. Their diluted EPS jumped by 32% to outpace the revenue growth rate comfortably and show that profitability is in a very solid position. Like the top line, EPS beat expectations as they surpassed estimates by $0.41. This strong profitability is also clearly demonstrated by the improvements in ROE and ROTCE. The respective 360 basis point and 410 basis point expansion in these two key metrics are very impressive, and I believe this quick overview shows that Morgan Stanley is firing on all cylinders right now.

Segment Analysis

Within Morgan Stanley's largest segment by revenue, there was broad strength. Institutional Securities' 19% increase in revenues shown above is actually a bit misleading since there were realized gains on sales of corporate loans held-for-sale in the year-ago quarter. The real performance of this segment is considerably stronger than what the headline figure suggests.

Firstly, for Investment Banking, the firm saw revenues increase by 36% YoY. This is a deceleration from 47% growth in Q4, but given how volatile markets have been, I would say the resilience we have seen has been exceptional. For both Equity and Fixed Income trading, the tailwinds from market volatility are clear. For the former, growth accelerated from 10% to 25%, while for the latter, there was improvement from a 9% decline in the previous quarter to 29% growth. For a firm that's not best known for its trading business, these are very robust results that clearly powered their strong Q1.

While Institutional Securities is the largest segment by revenues, there is little doubt that their Wealth Management business has become the crown jewel for Morgan Stanley. In Q1, this segment posted revenue growth of 16% YoY, which is also a speedup from Q4. Asset management, transactional, and net interest revenues all jumped YoY as another sign of broad strength. While these top-line numbers are important, a look at their total assets and flows should give an even better picture of how this all-important business is performing in this environment.

Morgan Stanley's Wealth Management segment ended Q1 with $2.792 trillion in fee-based client assets, up 19% YoY. They saw stronger fee-based asset inflows than in the prior year period by a good margin, and they also saw robust net new assets. As a whole, it is clear that Morgan Stanley continues to attract ultra-high-net-worth individuals, and so the momentum of this business really isn't showing many signs of slowing down right now.

Their Investment Management segment is by far the smallest by revenue, and there were some slightly mixed results for this business. Net revenues dropped by 4% YoY in Q1, with lowered accrued carried interest weighing on their performance-based income. However, asset management and related fees held quite steady to show resilience. In terms of AUM, there was actually a sizeable $221 billion increase YoY, and they also still saw $3.3 billion in long-term net inflows as a positive sign that this business has continued to expand.

Recent Opportunities & Risks

In recent times, some opportunities and risks have popped up for Morgan Stanley, and I believe they are worth mentioning here. Firstly, at the end of March, it was reported that their E*Trade platform is set to lead the SpaceX IPO for smaller investors. With the SpaceX IPO being undoubtedly the largest this year and retail investors eager to jump into another Musk company, Morgan Stanley's E*Trade could see a surge in popularity as we head towards the IPO. Furthermore, SpaceX is reportedly considering cutting out Robinhood (HOOD) and SoFi (SOFI) from retail roles, and so it is becoming increasingly clear that E*Trade will be the primary platform on which small investors could get allocated some shares before the stock starts to trade.

There is little question right now that private credit is one of the hottest topics in the financial sector. While some are racing to liquidate, Morgan Stanley seems to have other plans. Earlier this month, it was reported that the company is aiming to launch the North Haven Strategic Credit Fund. As Mary Christine Joy of Seeking Alpha writes

The interval fund is expected to offer single access to a wide spectrum of credit strategies, including private corporate loans, private hybrid financing solutions, private asset-based loans, private asset-backed lending facilities, broadly syndicated loans, high-yield bonds, securitized assets, and emerging market debt.

While the private credit space has historically been a very lucrative one, I believe this move by Morgan Stanley creates both risks and opportunities. If they are able to pick up attractive assets amid the fear, they could be in a position to deliver strong long-term returns. However, if this private credit crisis expands and leads to something like a 2008-style situation, this new fund may increase Morgan Stanley's exposure.

Like it or not, the crypto space has just gotten too large for major financial players to miss out. Earlier this month, Morgan Stanley launched their Bitcoin Trust (MSBT) in an attempt to attract investors that are looking for easy ways to gain exposure to the cryptocurrency. One of the most attractive aspects of this ETF is the fact that annual fees are just 0.14%, which is lower than BlackRock's (BLK) Bitcoin ETF (IBIT). Therefore, Morgan Stanley has introduced a very competitive product that should help them to gain traction in this space.

Less than a week ago, a significant risk has arisen for financial institutions. Anthropic released their Claude Mythos Preview, and many are worried about the cybersecurity risk that may come. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell summoned top Wall Street leaders, including Morgan Stanley CEO Ted Pick, for an urgent meeting. As Arundhati Sarkar of Seeking Alpha writes,

Bessent and Powell assembled the group at Treasury’s headquarters in Washington on Tuesday to make sure banks are aware of possible future risks raised by Anthropic’s Mythos and potential similar models and are taking precautions to defend their systems.

Moving forward, firms like Morgan Stanley are at great risk of AI cyberattacks, and in order to upgrade their defenses, the big banks and other financial institutions are likely going to have to carve out a significant budget.

Valuation is Elevated

There is a lot going right for Morgan Stanley currently, but at the same time, you can see that the P/B ratio for the firm has rebounded recently to a level that's far above both the 3-year median and the 5-year median for the multiple. I believe this shows that Morgan Stanley only remains a hold despite the impressive fundamentals.

Today, it was shown that their Institutional Securities segment and Wealth Management segment are both performing in a robust manner. Investment banking growth was resilient, trading performance was impressive, and ultra-high-net-worth individuals continue to trust Morgan Stanley with their money. While there are risks like private credit exposure and cybersecurity threats, the firm is firing on all cylinders with profitability ratios expanding.

Given the fundamentals laid out above, I would say that Morgan Stanley does deserve to be trading at a premium. However, with the P/B ratio now at one of the highest levels above its 3-year and 5-year medians in recent memory, I would say that some caution would be justified. A great business may not be overly attractive as an investment if the valuation already prices in spotless execution.

Final Thoughts

In my previous article, I upgraded Morgan Stanley to a hold rating from sell, and I believe that after today's analysis, it would be best to hang on to that hold call. The firm's fundamentals and performance are impressive to say the least, but with the valuation being where it is right now, it seems the market is clearly aware that Morgan Stanley is an excellent business. With the P/B being so far above the historic median, I would say that there is little opportunity left at this stage and that the risk/reward at this point is just around neutral.
